excerpt_en: April 26th. So, I held the walking event that I've been organizing in Dublin! Believe it or not, I've been keeping it going since August 2024, and we've managed 9 consecutive months of events. In the early days, I was holding them every weekend,
body_en:
April 26th

So, I held the walking event that I've been organizing in Dublin!

Believe it or not, I've been keeping it going since August 2024,

and we've managed 9 consecutive months of events.

In the early days, I was holding them every weekend,

but now I'm only in Ireland about once a week, so I hold them whenever I'm around.

excerpt_en: So, I stayed overnight at the airport, got into the Dublin Airport lounge at 4 AM, had breakfast and a morning Guinness, then took the 7 AM flight for about 3 hours. I arrived in Riga, the capital of Latvia! Let me give a quick overview of Latvia here.
body_en:
So, I stayed overnight at the airport,

got into the Dublin Airport lounge at 4 AM,

had breakfast and a morning Guinness,

then took the 7 AM flight for about 3 hours.

I arrived in Riga, the capital of Latvia!

Let me give a quick overview of Latvia here:

  • Country name: Republic of Latvia
  • Capital: Riga
  • Population: Approximately 1.9 million
  • Currency: Euro (€)
  • Language: Latvian (young people often speak English too)
  • Religion: Christianity (mainly Lutheran)
  • Time difference: 6-7 hours behind Japan (daylight saving time applies)
  • Safety: Relatively safe. Just watch out for pickpockets and scams
  • Located in the center of the Baltic States, bordered by Estonia to the north, Lithuania to the south, Russia to the east, and the Baltic Sea to the west
  • No direct flights from Japan, but accessible via European cities

#

  1. Beautiful Capital Riga
  • The medieval Old Town is a UNESCO World Heritage Site
  • Known as the "Paris of the Baltics" for its abundance of Art Nouveau architecture

2.

  1. A Country Rich in Nature
  • Forests cover more than half the country! Plenty of lakes and rivers too -- camping and canoeing in summer, skiing in winter

4.

  1. A Crossroads of History
  • Has a history of being ruled by Germany, Sweden, Russia, and the Soviet Union, whose influences remain in architecture and food culture

6.

  1. Gentle People and Quiet Living
  • Fewer tourists compared to other European destinations, creating a calm atmosphere
  • A country with a good balance of nature, culture, and daily life

Furthermore, May 4th is "Latvia's Independence Restoration Day" (the day in 1990 when Latvia re-declared independence from the Soviet Union) and is an important national holiday.

It just so happened that this day was Independence Day,

and memorial ceremonies, outdoor concerts, and other events were being held around the city.

In Riga, parades and music events are held around the Freedom Monument every year around May 4th, and the city is wrapped in a celebratory mood.

From what I'd researched beforehand,

public transportation was supposed to be free on the day, and some roads in the city center might be closed for events.

And sure enough, the bus from the airport to the city takes about 40 minutes, and no ticket was needed!!

First, I headed to the hotel to drop off my luggage.

As usual, it was a dormitory room.

Hotels are just for sleeping,

so there's no need to pay extra for a private room.

Despite being right in the center of Riga,

it was only 25,000 yen for 9 nights -- so cheap!

I'm really grateful!!

Then I quickly took a shower,

got myself together, and headed out.

Since it was Independence Day, there were

festivities going on at the nearby square.

After watching for a bit,

it was time for work.

The reason I came to Latvia is,

of course, for work purposes.

That's right -- to take Masu Photo pictures.

From January to April this year, I was traveling around Europe, but I wasn't doing any shoots.

The reason is simple: it was too cold, and I figured not many people would come out for photo shoots.

But now that it's May, it's gotten somewhat warmer,

so I came at this timing when shooting is possible.

So,

this time too, I went out for a Masu Photo shoot with a local model I'd been in touch with beforehand.

Michael.

He's someone who works as a model!

He was incredibly handsome.

What I changed in my approach this time is

that I removed the restriction of only shooting young women.

In order to expand the accessory brand "FOMUS PARURE," I want people of slightly older ages to wear the accessories too,

and on top of that, I've set up an art gallery in Hiroshima

where I display photos taken overseas,

but there were too many young women,

and I felt it was about time to include men as well.

Well, of course women are still the main focus,

but I think incorporating new approaches is necessary.

So,

I did a male model shoot for the first time in a while.

And then,

I had one more shoot today.

A female model from Latvia.

I'd heard beforehand that she couldn't wear the accessories, so we did a standard Masu Photo shoot.

This Anna,

she understood that I had come to Riga without knowing much about it or Latvia,

and after the shoot, we went to a cafe together

where she told me all sorts of recommendations for Riga.

Even though it was our first time meeting,

the kind of people who come to a Masu Photo project are all very outgoing and have great communication skills,

which makes things like this possible.

We also went to a supermarket together,

and she taught me about all kinds of things.

The cost of living is cheaper than Dublin,

so I thought it was really nice, haha.

So,

the schedule was that shooting started less than 2 hours after arriving in Latvia,

but I managed to wrap everything up successfully.

After that,

I went to a restaurant she recommended for dinner.

It's called Lido,

a pay-by-weight style restaurant.

The system is that the more you take, the more you pay.

The food was delicious!

I also got to have Latvian beet soup.

After warming up quite a bit,

I went back to the square

to check out the Independence Day festivities.

I didn't have any particular plans after that,

and I was interested in the Latvian performances,

so I stayed to listen,

and it was way better than I expected!!

Back in Japan, I used to go to live shows regularly,

but I hadn't been to any lately,

so I guess my body was craving a live performance.

I had an amazing time!!

Coming to Latvia and getting to see the

Independence Day festivities on my very first day

thanks to the perfect timing --

this was

such an incredibly fun experience for me!!

So,

from here, my activities in Latvia continue.

I have 2 more shoots lined up for tomorrow,

and things are off to a smooth start.

I'll keep at it!

excerpt_en: May 5th. So, once again I'm doing Masu Photo shoots from the morning. The shoots in Latvia also basically involve local models. Come to think of it, I hadn't explained about Masu Photo in this third series of articles. What is Masu Photo, and
body_en:
May 5th

So, once again I'm doing Masu Photo shoots from the morning.

The shoots in Latvia also

basically involve local models.

Come to think of it, I hadn't explained about Masu Photo in this third series of articles.

What is Masu Photo?

"Masu Photo" is a special photography style proposed by FOMUS, using the traditional Japanese wooden vessel "masu."

  • The subject holds or wears a "masu" during the shoot
  • The masu isn't just a prop -- it's used as a symbol that connects people
  • You don't have to be a professional model! Anyone can express their individuality through masu
  • Photos are taken in various locations across Japan and around the world, and are basically delivered on the same day

What is a Masu Photo Book?

A "Masu Photo Book" is a collection of works featuring these Masu Photos.

  • Masu Photos taken in Japan and around the world are compiled by theme
  • Through the photos, you can feel each person's charm, as well as the scenery and culture of each location
  • Photo books are published in e-book (Kindle), digital book, and NFT formats
  • Some photo books are available for free online viewing

To put it simply:

  • Masu Photo = A camera that captures "individuality" from around the world
  • Masu Photo Book = A travel album packed with those records

So that's pretty much the gist of it.

So far, I've done shoots in 12 countries,

and Latvia makes the 13th.

For this round of Masu Photo shoots in Latvia,

I'm planning to create a full photo book.

That's why I want to shoot at least 8 or more models.

I'm using every means possible to spread the word about Masu Photo

and shooting together with models living locally.

So,

on day 2 as well, I shot with 2 people.

The first was a woman who will be turning 50 next year.

She was a very active person

and really enjoyed the Masu Photo shoot.

She used to work in a military office,

and now she's also working as a lawyer.

On top of that, she's been doing modeling work recently,

and she had the same energy as the lively Japanese business owners I've met.

It was really fun talking with her.

And that's exactly why we got great Masu Photos.

After the shoot, we had lunch together.

She treated me to Latvian cuisine!

It was a really fun time!

And the second person of the day was a man.

He was born in India and had lived in the US,

and now he's living in Latvia for work.

His main job is as an engineer,

and on top of that, he's an influencer with a large following!

I thought it was interesting and had him participate in a Masu Photo shoot.

By the way, for finding models,

I use Instagram,

posting that if you want to be a Masu Photo model,

send me a message,

and from there, I carefully select the people I shoot with.

In other words, not just anyone can participate.

So,

this time was another really fun shoot!

Like this, it's a steady, grassroots effort,

but while flying around the world,

I'm having people directly experience FOMUS content while also spreading the word on social media,

gradually growing the fan base.

A spot where you can see all of Riga

After all the shoots were done,

the weather was really nice that day,

so I headed to St. Peter's Church.

There's a tower here where you can get a panoramic view of Riga,

and since I didn't know when the next nice day would come,

I went up on just my second day.

The entrance fee was 9 euros.

So here's the stunning view.

You can see the entire Riga cityscape.

It was clear, so the view was absolutely beautiful.

Actually,

in the Masu Photo books,

in addition to the masu models,

I also photograph FOMUS's official character,

Masu-Game.

Photos taken here will be included in the photo book.

So,

while I was shooting Masu-Game,

someone said "Let me take a photo of you too,"

and I got my picture taken, haha.

The iPhone 16 Pro takes really great photos! Haha.

So, like this,

after the shoots I wandered around the town of Riga.

It was a really productive day!

title_en: A Whirlwind of Photo Editing & AI Studying
excerpt_en: May 6th. My stay in Latvia has already reached day three. Today there are no outdoor shooting schedules, so I'm enjoying a somewhat relaxed morning for the first time in a while. That said, it's not a complete day off -- I'm spending quiet time carefully editing the photos of the 4 models I shot over the past two days, one by one.
body_en:
May 6th

My stay in Latvia has

already reached day three.

Today there are no outdoor shooting schedules, so I'm enjoying a somewhat relaxed morning for the first time in a while. That said, it's not a complete day off -- I'm spending quiet time carefully editing the photos of the 4 models I shot over the past two days, one by one.

Normally, you'd think this kind of work could be efficiently outsourced to AI. Indeed, recent AI image correction technology has made remarkable progress, and things like skin texture correction and retouching can be done almost automatically and naturally. I've been actively incorporating AI features for that part, and it's been a huge time-saver.

However, when it comes to color tones, brightness, and the overall atmosphere of the tone, that's a different story.

The "finish" I'm looking for is built on a delicate balance that can't be fully expressed through numbers or logic -- it's largely dependent on my own intuition and feeling.

A finish that's not too perfect, one that has a kind of "wavering" to it. Where the light goes a bit too far, where shadows remain, the raw quality of skin texture -- that kind of "imperfection" is, to me, part of what makes a photo charming and gives it personality.

That's precisely why editing is just as important a part of the creative process as shooting itself.

It takes time, but this process of trusting my own eyes and senses while deeply engaging with each photo is absolutely essential.

After finishing the photo editing,

I took a break

and went to a Latvian restaurant.

A different Latvian restaurant from the first day.

4 dishes for 12 euros.

About 1,900 yen.

That's a bit pricey, haha.

I should probably not order so much,

but everything looks so delicious,

and since it's buffet-style,

I end up grabbing too much, haha.

But the food is genuinely delicious!!

However, if I keep this up,

food costs will add up,

so I'm feeling like I need to switch to cooking mode at some point, haha.

Learning AI

Alongside my work as a photographer, I'm also running FOMUS, which I founded, and involved in various other projects.

One of them is consulting and support related to AI utilization. Simply put,

it's the role of "teaching someone how to use AI."

However, I think this isn't simply about being someone who "explains features" -- it's also about conveying the deeper "essence" behind it all.

Teaching someone means guiding them to the best solutions while understanding the challenges they face and the vision they want to achieve.

To do that, I first need to deeply understand AI myself and be able to use it flexibly. That's why I make an extra effort to "use AI to the fullest."

AI isn't a magic tool -- it's a tool whose value changes dramatically depending on the user's imagination and purpose. It's not just a "convenient tool" but a powerful partner for expanding the scope of expression and work.

For example, AI has already become indispensable in photo development and retouching. But the final touch -- the "finishing" that captures a person's essence -- I believe that's something only humans can do.

That's why being able to "use" AI and being able to "leverage" it are similar but completely different things. What I'm aiming for is the latter.

Rather than just an instruction manual, I'd like to help people find "a way of using it that suits who they are."

So yeah, studying AI is a must.

In reality, it's been incredibly useful for my own work, so just being able to use AI well is a genuine strength.

So,

I actually wrote a Kindle book.

https://a.co/37JEZiX

It's about 30,000 characters, and I wrote it in almost one day.

This book is for people who want to learn ChatGPT

but don't know where to start,

or who want to know what it can do.

What I teach my clients is more flexible and tailored to their specific needs,

so it's a bit different from this book's content, but it covers the basics.

I made it hoping it would be at least somewhat helpful.

That said, aside from the "Introduction" and "Conclusion," the AI wrote most of it, so I barely wrote anything myself, haha.

This was created by AI while I was editing photos.

My work efficiency multiplied several times over, haha.

Studying AI really never goes to waste!!

excerpt_en: May 7th. Today the shoot starts at 8 AM! The model said this time slot worked for her, so I got up early too. I'm getting used to my stay in Latvia, and I've started to more or less figure out the routes through the Old Town.
body_en:
May 7th

Today the shoot starts at 8 AM!

The model said this time slot worked for her, so I got up early too.

I'm getting used to my stay in Latvia,

and I've started to more or less figure out the routes through the Old Town.

Today's model was

an Indian woman who also works as an engineer.

On top of that,

she wore a traditional Indian outfit, a sari, for the shoot.

This week in Latvia was really cold,

and yet she went out of her way to wear a sari --

I couldn't be more grateful.

The shoot took about 30 minutes,

and it was a really fun session!

The finished photos will be

published later as a Masu Photo book!

After the shoot,

I went back to the hotel for a short break.

After that,

I headed to the library across the river in Riga

that one of the models had told me about.

It was about a 15-minute walk.

To the Cafe

There was apparently a cafe there,

so I went for lunch.

This was also Latvian style.

You order what you want and they pile it on your plate.

This came to about 10 euros.

So about 1,700 yen.

It ended up being a bit pricey, haha.

But it was delicious, so all good!!

Then I walked back to the hotel,

got ready, and headed out for shoot number 2!

The second Masu Photo session was with Riza, a professional local model.

She's from Latvia and has modeling experience

in Georgia and other countries too.

She was very easy-going,

and I had a great time talking with her!

When the conversation flows, you get great Masu Photos.

Communication with the other person is incredibly important

for Masu Photo shoots overseas too, so I make sure to be upbeat and friendly myself.

That's the secret to taking great photos.

So, it was another fun shoot!

To the Central Market

After the shoot, I headed to the Central Market.

I'd heard it was Riga's biggest market, so I went to check it out, and wow -- the beer was incredibly cheap!

A beer after a shoot is the best!!

excerpt_en: May 8th. Since I had no shoots scheduled for the whole day, I took a train from Riga Station and headed to a place called Sigulda, about an hour away. This is a place that doesn't show up on Japanese tourism sites. The reason I ended up here was that all the Latvian models unanimously recommended
body_en:
May 8th

Since I had no shoots scheduled for the whole day,

I took a train from Riga Station and headed to a place called Sigulda, about an hour away.

This is a place that doesn't show up on Japanese tourism sites.

The reason I ended up here was that

all the Latvian models unanimously

recommended this place called "Sigulda."

If they're recommending it,

I have no choice but to go,

so I actually came here, haha.

I'm extremely quick on my feet when it comes to things like this.

So,

when I looked into Sigulda,

I found out there are 3 major landmarks,

so I decided to visit each one.

First up, the castle ruins.

It was about a 20-minute walk to get there.

Sigulda Medieval Castle Ruins

Ruins of a castle built in the 13th century.

There was a watchtower-like building nearby,

so I climbed it.

And since it was the first place I'd been in a while where there was absolutely nobody around, I recorded an episode of the video podcast I recently started.

Going forward,

I feel the need to build my outreach capabilities,

so whenever I have time, I'm planning to record solo talks

of about 10 to 20 minutes in length.

The great thing about video podcasts is that

they can be published on YouTube, Spotify, and if you transcribe them, you can even repost the text on platforms like note.

A single recording can be adapted across multiple platforms -- that's the strength.

So I decided to give this new challenge a try.

From the castle, I walked about another 20 minutes

to the next stop: Gutmanis Cave.

Gutmanis Cave

Gutmanis Cave (Gutmana ala) is the largest natural cave in the Baltic States, located in Sigulda, Latvia, and is an important spot that symbolizes the trinity of Latvian history, folklore, and tourism.

Since it's quite far from Riga,

there weren't that many tourists, which made it easy to see.

I did some video recording here too.

Once again, being able to film videos anywhere

is really convenient since I can do it in my spare time.

The remaining question is whether I can talk for about 15 minutes.

That's going to take practice.

I just have to keep doing it.

Then,

I walked about another 30 minutes to Turaida Castle.

Turaida Castle

Turaida Castle is a representative historical spot in Sigulda, Latvia.

  • Year built: 1214 (early 13th century)
  • Architectural style: Medieval red brick Gothic
  • Admission: Around 6 euros for adults

Historical Background:

  • Turaida means "God's Garden" and was once a sacred site of the Livs (an indigenous Baltic people).
  • In 1214, it was built by the Livonian Order on the orders of Archbishop Albert of Riga.
  • For several centuries after, it served as a religious and military stronghold.
  • It fell into ruin after an 18th-century fire, was restored from the 20th century onward, and is now a cultural heritage site with an attached museum.

So they say.

It looked like this!

Getting up there was quite a steep climb too.

I got to see all sorts of things.

This kind of trip isn't just sightseeing --

it's a proper research visit.

In order to create the character called KUKU,

I travel the world while expanding my creative imagination.

This is the kind of thing where inspiration only comes

because you actually go there,

so I'm exploring with all of that in mind.

This castle was really wonderful too!

And for the trip back, I took the bus.

It was less than 1 euro! Haha.

I walked over 60 minutes from the station to the castle,

but by bus it's only about 10 minutes.

After that, I went to a food court in Sigulda

and had a kebab.

Since I had nothing left to do,

I took the train back to Riga a little after 5.

It was just a day trip, but I had a great time,

so if you ever go to Latvia,

definitely consider adding Sigulda to your list!

title_en: The Accessories Used in Masu Photo Shoots
excerpt_en: Day 6 of my Latvia stay. Once again, I have 2 Masu Photo shoots scheduled today. This overseas activity diary is published as paid content, and it also serves as my personal record, but the biggest weakness is that when you keep doing the same things over and over, you run out of material to write about.
body_en:
Day 6 of my Latvia stay.

Once again, I have 2 Masu Photo shoots scheduled today.

This overseas activity diary is

published as paid content,

and it also serves as my personal record,

but the biggest weakness is that when you keep doing the same things over and over,

you run out of material to write about.

Right now in Latvia,

I'm here to create

a Masu Photo book,

and while the models I'm shooting

are all unique and interesting people,

the articles end up being

pretty much the same every time.

So this time,

rather than the usual "shot this, talked about that,"

I'd like to dig a little deeper into the item that brings out the most "FOMUS-ness" in the shoots -- the masu jewelry "FOMUS PARURE."

What is FOMUS PARURE?

FOMUS PARURE is FOMUS's original masu jewelry brand, born from the concept of "wearing culture as fashion."

This jewelry is not just decorative accessories.

By using the traditional Japanese craft of "masu" as a material and fusing it with modern fashion and artistic sensibilities,

each piece is designed as a "cultural accessory" with its own story.

The material used is small wooden pieces of masu, made from beautifully grained, fragrant hinoki (Japanese cypress).

These are carefully polished by artisans to achieve both durability and lightness, then combined with metals and natural stones to create earrings, clip-on earrings, pendants, and more.

The Role in Shoots

During this round of Latvia shoots as well, several models wore the FOMUS PARURE earrings.

The texture of the wood and the soft light accentuate each model's individuality, radiating a "quiet presence" through the viewfinder.

What's interesting is that local people always ask,

"What is that?"

From there, I explain, "This is made from a traditional Japanese craft called 'masu,'" and a natural cultural exchange unfolds.

In other words, FOMUS PARURE is also

"jewelry that makes people want to start a conversation."

Future Plans

Going forward, I want to strengthen FOMUS PARURE's presence as "jewelry that lets you wear culture."

Beyond using it in shoots, I'm also looking at selling through FOMUS's e-commerce site and at local events, as well as offering limited items to subscription members.

Each piece is only truly "complete" when the person who wears it connects it with their own sensibility and background --

I want to keep spreading that kind of jewelry to street corners around the world.

So that was a brief re-introduction to FOMUS's jewelry business, "FOMUS PARURE."

Both Masu Photo shoots today were really fun!

They were properly wearing the FOMUS PARURE too!

title_en: Finally Meeting Japanese People in Latvia
excerpt_en: So, it's been a week since I arrived in Latvia! Time really flies. Today I had a shoot at 1 PM, so I was doing administrative work during the daytime. For lunch, I went to the McDonald's near my hotel.
body_en:
So, it's been a week since I arrived in Latvia!

Time really flies.

Today I had a shoot at 1 PM,

so I was doing administrative work during the daytime.

For lunch, I went to

the McDonald's near my hotel.

I've ended up visiting McDonald's all over the world, and the menu and prices differ by country.

For example, in Ireland,

ketchup is free,

but in Latvia everything costs extra, and so on.

The little differences between countries

are pretty interesting.

Masu Photo Shoot

Off to the Masu Photo shoot.

Shooting in Latvia has been

incredibly stress-free,

because everyone actually shows up on time!

This is not something you can take for granted at all.

Even among Japanese people, some are pretty lax about it,

and in the Philippines,

hardly any of the models showed up on time, haha.

And yet, the Latvian models

all show up right on schedule,

which is seriously the best, haha.

So, I had a shoot with Klara.

She's a working professional who is also

attending school.

From what she told me,

I could tell she's incredibly multi-talented.

Masu Photo models are all a little unique,

and I love that those are the kinds of people who show up!

I'd usually been shooting in the Old Town,

but having the same background every time isn't great,

so I shot on the other side of the Old Town near the library I'd visited before.

Mixing things up is important too.

Once again, I got some lovely Masu Photos.

Saturday Market

After the shoot, I wandered around town.

The weather was nice, so I checked out various places.

I ended up at the Central Market.

But of course, being Saturday,

the food court was absolutely packed with people.

There didn't seem to be anywhere to sit,

so I ended up leaving without having a single drink, haha.

Japanese People Were There

I had video podcast editing and other tasks piling up,

and while I was working away in the hotel's common living area,

I heard Japanese being spoken.

Would you believe it -- two Japanese people

happened to be staying at the same hotel.

I'm usually in my room working by around 8 PM,

but being in the shared living room led to this unexpected encounter, haha.

I hadn't used Japanese once

since coming to Latvia, so it felt refreshing, haha.

Since we were there, I chatted with them for a bit.

One was a traveler touring Europe from Japan, staying in Latvia for just a few days.

Apparently Latvia was their 99th country! Haha.

The other person was in Latvia

on a working holiday.

The fact that they came all the way to Latvia means

they're not exactly on the typical tourist route,

so everyone's a bit quirky, haha.

I'm pretty far off the beaten path myself,

so just like the masu models, among Japanese people too, the slightly quirky ones are the most interesting,

which is perfect for me, haha.

My Latvia stay is entering the second half now,

but every day here is full of new encounters and discoveries,

and it's truly inspiring.

2 more days to go.

I'll keep having fun while spreading FOMUS even further!!

excerpt_en: May 12th. All the shoots in Latvia are done, and today is a full day off. Since I had the chance, I decided to take a trip to Rundale Palace, which is about a 2-hour bus ride each way from Riga. *Rundale Palace (Rundales pils) is a magnificent Baroque-style palace located in southern Latvia, a historical building from the 18th century.
body_en:
May 12th

All the shoots in Latvia are done,

and today is a full day off.

Since I had the chance, I decided to take a trip to Rundale Palace, which is about a 2-hour bus ride each way from Riga.

*Rundale Palace (Rundales pils) is a magnificent Baroque-style palace located in southern Latvia, a historical building from the 18th century. It's known as one of the premier tourist spots in the Baltic States.

On weekdays, there are only 2 direct buses per day from Riga, so I had to plan the schedule carefully in advance. It's a bit of a long trip, but it was more than worth it.

When I actually arrived, I was overwhelmed by its grandeur. It felt like stepping into a painting. Not just the exterior, but the interior decorations and every little detail were like works of art. As I went from room to room, I couldn't help but imagine the lives of the aristocrats who once lived there.

What was even more impressive were the vast gardens. Apparently the rose garden is at its best in July, but it was still too early for roses during my visit. Even so, the tulips in full bloom were magnificent, and the spring colors filled my heart with joy.

I had about 3 hours at the palace since I needed to catch the return bus, but that was more than enough to enjoy it fully. If I ever get the chance, I'd love to visit again when the roses are in bloom.

Drinks with Fellow Japanese

On the last night, I had a toast with the

2 Japanese people who happened to be at the same hotel.

Meeting in Latvia of all places

felt like it must have been fate.

It turned out to be a really fun time.

The sense of accomplishment from finishing the shoots, the lingering beauty of Rundale Palace, and this chance encounter.

With all these events overlapping,

my last night in Latvia was warm

and an unforgettable moment.

Travel really is fascinating.

title_en: The Art-Filled "Republic of Uzupis" and the Connections FOMUS Creates
excerpt_en: May 13, 2025 -- Arrival in Lithuania. After wrapping up activities in Latvia, I moved on to my next destination, Lithuania. A daytime bus ride of about 4 hours. The fare was only about 1,500 yen -- what a bargain. This time, the purpose is FOMUS and the "Republic of Uzupis."
body_en:
May 13, 2025 -- Arrival in Lithuania

After wrapping up activities in Latvia, I moved on to my next destination, Lithuania.

A daytime bus ride of about 4 hours.

The fare was only about 1,500 yen -- what a bargain.

This Time, the Purpose Is FOMUS and the "Republic of Uzupis"

I had 2 main reasons for coming to Lithuania:

  • Promotional activities for "FOMUS," which brings the traditional Japanese craft of masu to the world
  • And a visit to the "Republic of Uzupis," known as a "mysterious independent nation"

The Republic of Uzupis: A Land of Art and Freedom

When I actually walked through Uzupis,

the entire town felt like a "living gallery."

Poetry carved into walls, unique art installations lined up, and an air of freedom.

This wasn't just a tourist spot -- it was a place where art and ideology coexist, like its own country.

The Old Town of Vilnius Is Charming Too

During my stay, a local FOMUS model showed me around,

and we explored Vilnius's Old Town and historical spots.

Being able to learn about the culture and history directly from a local

is a precious experience you can't easily get on a regular trip.

The Long-Lasting Connections Born from Masu Photo

The people I meet through FOMUS's "Masu Photo" become more than just

"photography models" -- lasting bonds are formed.

For example, photos from shoots get displayed in a Japanese art gallery (#Sagishima Art Gallery),

and when models visit Japan, we share recommendations for places to visit.

#I'll be introducing friends' shops

In that way, FOMUS becomes a hub for exchange between people.

I feel that's where the true appeal of this project lies.

My stay in Lithuania has just begun.

I'm sure there will be

plenty of interesting encounters in this country too.

I'll keep traveling onward,

carrying the spirit of FOMUS with me!
